title: OAIC reporting compliance for eligible breaches

compliance.assurance_rationale: AU Privacy Act §26WK — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that AU Privacy Act §26WK (NDB — notifiable data breach) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.31.2: OAIC reporting compliance for eligible breaches.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.31.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, endpoint protection logs, cloud audit trails, vulnerability scan outputs, email security logs, network infrastructure logs, identity management systems, incident response records
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=grc sourcetype="breach:assessment" region="AU" earliest=-180d
| stats latest(decision) as d by breach_id
| where d="notifiable" AND isnull(oaic_reference)
description: Evidences Privacy Act 1988 (Cth); NDB obligations for oaic reporting compliance for eligible breaches using endpoint, identity, and cloud telemetry.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for AU Privacy Act compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes grc and sourcetypes breach:assessment. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ingest CrowdStrike policy posture via TA 5082 and map hosts to NDB and NZISM tiers. (2) Tune alerts with ACSD Essential Eight and APRA-aligned severities. (3) Coordinate privacy and security ownership for dual-reporting workflows. (4) Monthly export for privacy officer and prudential working groups. Indexes required: `index=grc`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=breach:assessment`.
visualization: Single value (% compliant hosts), Bar chart (fails by control), Table (worst hosts). Key fields: `breach_id`, `d`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.process_name, Processes.user, Processes.dest | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for evidences privacy act 1988 (cth); ndb obligations for oaic reporting compliance for eligible breaches using endpoint, identity, and cloud telemetry. This helps maintain steady compliance posture and reduces manual review effort.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, endpoint protection logs, cloud audit trails, vulnerability scan outputs, email security logs, network infrastructure logs, identity management systems, incident response records.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ingest CrowdStrike policy posture via TA 5082 and map hosts to NDB and NZISM tiers. (2) Tune alerts with ACSD Essential Eight and APRA-aligned severities. (3) Coordinate privacy and security ownership for dual-reporting workflows. (4) Monthly export for privacy officer and prudential working groups.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=grc sourcetype="breach:assessment" region="AU" earliest=-180d
| stats latest(decision) as d by breach_id
| where d="notifiable" AND isnull(oaic_reference)
```

Understanding this SPL

**OAIC reporting compliance for eligible breaches (Privacy Act 1988 (Cth); NDB)** — Evidences Privacy Act 1988 (Cth); NDB obligations for oaic reporting compliance for eligible breaches using endpoint, identity, and cloud telemetry.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, endpoint protection logs, cloud audit trails, vulnerability scan outputs, email security logs, network infrastructure logs, identity management systems, incident response records. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: grc; **sourcetype**: breach:assessment. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=grc, sourcetype="breach:assessment", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by breach_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Filters the current rows with `where d="notifiable" AND isnull(oaic_reference)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.process_name, Processes.user, Processes.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**OAIC reporting compliance for eligible breaches (Privacy Act 1988 (Cth); NDB)** — Evidences Privacy Act 1988 (Cth); NDB obligations for oaic reporting compliance for eligible breaches using endpoint, identity, and cloud telemetry.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, endpoint protection logs, cloud audit trails, vulnerability scan outputs, email security logs, network infrastructure logs, identity management systems, incident response records. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Endpoint.Processes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value (% compliant hosts), Bar chart (fails by control), Table (worst hosts).
